video_title: Investors Believe This. 200 Years of Data Doesn't Agree
gist: Financial historian Brian Taylor (Global Financial Data / Finyon) argues from nine centuries of cross-country stock/bond data that the equity risk premium does not reliably exist, that US bonds had negative real returns for ~70 years (early 1900s to 1981), and that returns are driven by his 'TWIG' framework (Trade, War, Inflation, Government) -- all four of which he grades as currently unfavorable in 2026.

themes.theme: TWIG framework as the driver of long-run returns

insights.claim: Long-run equity returns are driven by four factors -- Trade (free trade good), War (always bad), Inflation (worst enemy of fixed income), and Government intervention (bad) -- and the highest returns occur only when all four align favorably.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Gives a top-down macro checklist for grading whether a country's policy regime is set up for high or negative equity returns.
insights.quote: if there is a maximum of trade, no war, a minimum of inflation, and a minimum of government intervention, that's when investors get the highest returns possible.

insights.claim: The 1980s-1990s were a textbook all-cylinders-firing period (free trade, Reagan-era shrinking government, peace dividend, disinflation) producing two decades of double-digit US returns, while the 1910s/World War I was the inverse and one of the few decades of negative returns.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Anchors the framework in concrete historical regimes investors can pattern-match against today.

insights.claim: As of 2026 the TWIG factors are net unfavorable -- tariffs restrict trade, there is a war in Iran, and inflation is picking up -- with the government's promotion of AI the only clear positive.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: A bearish-leaning macro read: three of four return drivers are headwinds, arguing for caution on forward US return expectations.
insights.quote: current government policy is not following the twig theory... restrictions on trade through tariffs. We're currently involved with a war in Iran. Inflation is picking up.

insights.claim: Import-substitution economies that avoid trade (Brazil, Argentina) have historically delivered poor investor returns, while export-heavy economies (Switzerland, Singapore) have delivered strong ones.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Supports a structural bias toward open-trade economies when allocating across emerging and developed markets.

themes.theme: The equity risk premium is not an ironclad law

insights.claim: A stable, persistent equity risk premium does not really exist; stock and bond returns move independently, so apparent ERP spikes (e.g. after 2021's rate moves or post-WWII) are artifacts of bond returns changing, not a structural reward for equity risk.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Directly challenges a core CAPM/textbook assumption; argues for forecasting stock and bond returns separately rather than assuming stocks must beat bonds.
insights.quote: the return on bonds and the return on stocks move independently of one another. And so... you can't really say that there is a equity risk premium.

insights.claim: Equities have underperformed bonds over many 10-year windows -- and for decades even in the US -- making 'stocks always beat bonds' false over career-length horizons that matter to most investors.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor / Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Undercuts the assumption underpinning equity-heavy allocations; time horizon, not dogma, should drive the stock/bond mix.
insights.quote: there's no more ironclad law in all of investing that people believe that stocks outperform bonds.

insights.claim: Over 10-20 year horizons equities will probably outperform bonds, but over 2-3 year windows bonds can easily outperform equities -- the right allocation depends entirely on horizon and risk tolerance.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Reframes the stock/bond decision as horizon-dependent rather than a fixed law, relevant to liability-matched and shorter-duration investors.
insights.quote: Invest until you can sleep at night.

insights.claim: The cross-country, multi-century ERP analysis is available free in the Finyon (Finnegan) guide on SSRN covering 27 countries over two centuries, and almost no one else has done this specific analysis.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Points to a specific, free primary data source for investors who want to stress-test the ERP assumption themselves.

themes.theme: Bonds, interest-rate pyramids and the forward outlook

insights.claim: US bonds delivered negative real returns for roughly 70 years -- from the early 1910s to 1981 -- covering most of the 20th century, a fact most modern investors are unaware of.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Warns that the 1981-2021 bond bull market was an anomaly, not the baseline, recalibrating expectations for fixed income.
insights.quote: almost 70 years... the majority of the 20th century where US bonds had a negative... real return.

insights.claim: History shows two 'interest-rate pyramids' -- 1896-1920 and the larger 1941-1981 (2% to 15-16% on government bonds), each followed by a long decline; the 1981-2021 fall took the 10-year from ~15% to under 0.5% (negative in Europe and Japan).
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Frames rate regimes as multi-decade pyramids; the key risk to watch is the start of a third upward pyramid.

insights.claim: The current 10-year yield is itself the best simple predictor of a bond's forward return, because capital gains and losses from rate moves roughly offset the yield over the holding period.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Gives a clean rule of thumb: ~4% starting yield implies ~4% forward bond returns, so don't extrapolate 1981-2021 gains.
insights.quote: the current yield on the 10-year bond tells you basically what return you're going to get

insights.claim: Rates are unlikely to return to the ultra-low levels of the 2010s; the 10-year should stay around 4% (3% in Europe) and the Fed under Warsh is wary of hiking, so investors must reset expectations rather than rely on falling-yield tailwinds.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Implies the bond-return tailwind of declining yields is over; total returns must come from coupon, not price appreciation.
insights.quote: interest rates have jumped up to 4% on the 10-year bond... and they're not going back down.

insights.claim: A 30-year cycle has governed US stocks for a century -- double-digit return decades in the 1920s, 1950s, 1980s, 2010s -- implying the 2040s could be strong while the 2030s (following the bad 1940s/1970s/2000s pattern) may disappoint.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: A pattern-based caution flag for the upcoming 2030s decade; useful as a contrarian sentiment check, not a precise timing tool.
insights.quote: the 2040s are going to be providing great returns... that does not bode well for the 2030s.

insights.claim: You cannot analyze returns over one long blended period; you must break history into eras and identify which TWIG factors were encouraging or preventing returns, because where you 'clip' the right edge of a chart drives wildly different conclusions, a phenomenon called 'right-hand chart bias'.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor / Meb Faber
insights.so_what: Methodological warning against naive long-run extrapolation; claims based on a single end-date are fragile.

insights.claim: Investors who simply extrapolate the 1981-2020 era of declining yields and strong bond returns will reach the wrong forward conclusion; the regime has structurally changed and forecasts must be forward-looking.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Reinforces resetting both bond and balanced-portfolio return assumptions away from the recent past.
insights.quote: anyone who's just going to look at, well, what happened since 1981, they're going to get the wrong perspective.

themes.theme: Debt, market cap and dividend-yield myths

insights.claim: When a country's stock market capitalization exceeds its government debt it is generally positive for stocks, and vice versa; in the US both have risen since 1980 but market cap has risen faster -- a positive sign that would turn negative if it reversed.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Offers a relative gauge (market cap vs. government debt) to watch as a structural warning signal for equities.

insights.claim: US government debt now exceeds GDP, which was tolerable at near-zero rates in the 2010s but becomes a genuine concern now that rates are around 4%.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Higher rates raise the debt-servicing risk that could crowd out capital and pressure equity returns.

insights.claim: The falling dividend yield is misread as bearish; it has largely fallen because market capitalization has exploded to ~2x GDP -- adjusting for that, dividends-as-a-share-of-GDP show little real change over time.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Debunks the low-dividend-yield-as-warning narrative; yield compression reflects market growth, not deteriorating fundamentals.
insights.quote: you can't... continue to have a 6% dividend yield when the stock market capitalization is twice GDP

insights.claim: Until the 1960s US dividend yields were consistently above government bond yields, then crossed below and essentially never returned -- a structural break driven by the post-WWII rise of capital gains and the introduction of taxes (absent in the 1800s).
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Explains why pre-1960 valuation/yield relationships are not comparable to today's; tax regime changes investor behavior.

themes.theme: US dominance, concentration and Anglo markets

insights.claim: The Anglo countries (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand) have consistently controlled 70-80% of total world stock market capitalization throughout history, and the predicted emerging-market takeover of 20 years ago never materialized.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Argues against structural overweighting of emerging markets on the thesis they'll dominate global cap; Anglo dominance has been durable.
insights.quote: the Anglo countries... have consistently controlled 70 to 80% of the total stock market capitalization of the world.

insights.claim: US share of world market cap hit ~70% in the 1950s (Canada then 10%) and is again roughly 60-66% today; the US repeatedly re-leads by inventing the next growth wave -- computers, internet, and now AI, which is centered in the US, not Europe.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Supports a continued US equity overweight thesis tied to US capture of each technology revolution, with AI the current driver.

insights.claim: Top-10-company concentration in the US market is the highest ever, but a comparable 1950s-60s concentration did not cause a bubble collapse -- gains later broadened to the rest of the market, so concentration is not inherently a sell signal.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the popular 'concentration = imminent crash' narrative; historically it broadened rather than broke.

insights.claim: Markets reorder dramatically within a lifetime -- South Korea's stock market is now larger than the UK's, and Korean per-capita GDP now exceeds Japan's, largely driven by semiconductors.
insights.speaker: Meb Faber / Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Illustrates that national market leadership is non-stationary; long-horizon allocators should not assume today's hierarchy persists.

themes.theme: Tail risk: total wipeouts and currencies

insights.claim: Permanent -100% equity wipeouts are almost always government- or hyperinflation-induced; Russia (1917 October Revolution) was an unexpected total loss, while China (1937-1949) gave investors a 12-year warning during which Shanghai market cap fell from ~$1bn to ~$50m as smart money exited to Japan, Hong Kong and the US.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Frames the true tail risk for equities as political/regime risk, and shows capital flight signals often precede market closure.
insights.quote: in the case of Russia, after 1917, it was an unexpected event. People just got completely wiped out.

insights.claim: Short of a regime collapse, even 80-90% drawdowns from hyperinflation or depression eventually recover because equities track real economic value; the risk of a Russia/China-style total loss in major markets today is seen as very low.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Distinguishes recoverable drawdowns from permanent losses, supporting staying invested through severe but non-political bear markets.
insights.quote: as long as the real value of the economy recovers, which it inevitably will, you will eventually get your money back.

insights.claim: Over the past 100 years only the Swiss franc has outperformed the US dollar; developed currencies oscillate around a mean while developing-country currencies (especially Africa and South America) chronically depreciate -- Brazil and Argentina have run through six currencies in 30-40 years.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Currency stability is a screenable, regionally-clustered factor for global allocation and EM risk management.
insights.quote: really only the Swiss franc has outperformed the United States over the past 100 years.

themes.theme: Long-run equity revolution and investor culture

insights.claim: Global equity market cap rose from ~1% of GDP in 1800 to ~40% by WWII, stagnated at 40% from 1914 to 1981, then a second globalization-driven jump took it to 100-200% (US now ~2x GDP) -- the breakpoint aligns with Maddison's finding that per-capita GDP only began growing around 1820 as equity investing emerged.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Ties the entire equity-investing era to the post-1820 growth regime; the post-1981 cap surge is the main driver of recent wealth creation.

insights.claim: Investor behavior has evolved from a 1800s bond-and-income focus (British consols, French rentes), to individual stock-picking in the first half of the 1900s, to index funds and ETFs dominating in the 21st century -- and index ownership now drives the market.
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: Contextualizes passive-flow dominance as the latest stage of a centuries-long shift, relevant to debates on index-driven price formation.

insights.claim: All modern market techniques -- short selling, bear raids, options -- existed by the early 1600s; the Dutch East India (VOC) stock began trading in 1602 and Lemaire ran a bear raid by 1608, so 'there's nothing new under the sun.'
insights.speaker: Brian Taylor
insights.so_what: A reminder that speculative dynamics and bubbles (e.g. the 1720s) recur, useful framing for current bubble debates.
insights.quote: all of the techniques for the stock market, bear raids, options, all of those began back in the 1600s.
